author: @chrisbingham3289
text: The cost of a tool is also a big concern. The old saying "you get what you can pay for " plays a very big part in whether you have the right tool for the job at hand.

author: @illmnts
text: I know you can make bevels in Tinkercad but it really needs a button for bevelling joined shapes afterwards. It's not just aestheticially pleasing it saves time and material when printing.

text: Freecad is amazing. I built a 3d printer almost 10 years ago and learned free cad to make my own parts. Learning curve is somewhat steep but once you get the hang of it you can make anything.

author: @wimwiddershins
text:
I've been able to visualise and modify 3D objects in my head since I was a kid. Play-Doh, Lego, Mechano and pulling apart all my toys were all normal things to me.
I actually really enjoy using Solidworks, Freecad, and other packages. It's addictive and fun. Only gets frustrating when you bump up against software limitations, or features that have been poorly implemented.

author: @GlabrousDiver
text: For years I've made fag-packet sketches to show my Engineers and CAD Team a concept. On your advice I tried Tinkercad. I then moved on to Onshape...absolutely love it! I can now 3D print a mock-up and test functionality, prior to sending out a design for fabrication. You, sir, are an inspiration. Please keep up the great work.

author: @EngDrewman
text: Why is there no talk of FreeCAD? It does BOTH primitive shape modeling and parametric. Unlike Onshape, F360, and TinkerCAD, it's open source and free to use for both private and commercial use, no strings attached, you fully own your own work. FreeCAD is also multiplatform and is available for Windows, Linux, and Mac.

author: @stewiex
text:
I really enjoy doing 3d modeling. I tried to use AutoCAD many years ago, but found it very daunting. 

As I got older, I got into 3d printing and still intimidated from my AutoCAD experience, made a few things with Paint 3D (the program that ships with Windows). This was surprisingly easier than expected, so I gave Fusion 360 a try, loved it and never looked back.

Looking forward though, I also use OnShape now which is every bit as capable as Fusion 360 with the added benefit of not needing a beefy computer to run it.

Also if I could suggest one thing people  do when trying a new CAD program... Have something in mind you want to design. If you're mindlessly clicking on tools to figure out what they do with no goal in mind, you'll probably get bored and move on to something else. If you have something in mind to make, your only goal is to find the tool you need to make the thing you're picturing which will make it more of a creative and enjoyable experience in my opinion.

author: @roberthopgood1894
text:
NOT everyone brain functions that way
my mind is process oriented - i CAN NOT "see" in 3 dimensions
my brother does - when he starts a woodworking project he has it done in his mind in 3D

author: @HealthyDisrespectforAuthority
text:
I loved google sketchup.. lots of good tools, plugins and tons of online support. 
I'm not fond of trimble sketchup --the interface is less intuitive, harder to see and the community got snooty.
And I can't stand blender...mostly because of the interface with the teeny tiny tools in light grey on dark grey.  If ya can't see the tools, ya can't use the tools.
Shame I can't get the old google sketchup to work properly on my linux mint system.

text: autodidact lego lincoln logs erector sets then just quit college to work every trade into the digital era and now settling down on 20 acres of forested hills with a cnc workshop to spend my twiddling on the porch years. a pocket knife and a twig sure has evolved from my grandfathers day!!!

author: @ecatVids
text:
There are indeed loads of 'creators' singing the praises of OnShape. Rattling through the same list of key points and features, almost as if they are all reading from the same script.

The cynic in me screams undeclared sponsored content. I'm willing to give Robert the benefit of the doubt here, but other channels are certainly leaving or have left a bad taste in my mouth.

While this may not be important to you, remember, you do not have *ownership* of anything stored in 'the cloud'.

author: @UnmitigatedMind
text: Thank you for your informative videos!

but there is sometimes something in between like Ondsel ES: built on freeCad but without the brainf*x this is the next software i'm going to put some time in.. curious though where this all goes

author: @IowaKeith
text: I started learning tinkercad because of your 3d printed generator videos.  I've gotten fairly comfortable with it and have replicated many of your designs.  I recently started trying to learn onshape, to make some designs i have come up with.  I do have to say, with Onshape I am having to make way more baby steps to figure this out.  It's definitely a learning process.

author: @markrumrey790
text: I have been a big fan of Fusion 360, a greater learning curve than Tinker, but much greater results.

author: @JFSmith-nb8hf
text: I was a machinist for 40 years. When someone hands you a three view drawing of a complicated part, with no isometric view, you have learn to think 3 dimensionality pretty quick.😆

replies.text: The pressure is on in that case, eh?

replies.author: @JFSmith-nb8hf
replies.text: Do the chore, or out the door.​@thinkshesapenguin1o166

author: @Ammoniummetavanadate
text: I found spending the money on a 3d mouse really helped with my workflow in fusion360. It wasn't cheap but it makes navigation in 3d feel way more natural.

author: @grendel1960a
text: totally agree, learn the basics of what you are trying to do and start doing, I have 34 years CAD experience- and I am still learning every day- I too was bought up with stuff like meccano, plus a shorter section of work bench in my dads shed and my own tools, I did my own bicycle repairs and later motorbikes and cars. dont try and learn cad by learning how to do everything at once- it wont work, start simple and work up you will learn more by getting hands on. and visualising a 3d part to put it into CAD - thats not easy, using building blocks like in tinkercad is the way to go, just building things up from simple shapes, then cutting other shapes out to get the finished result you want is a great process, thats how I work, and I have been in the industry for a long time. I am the first to admit I dont know everything about CAD. if you get the result you want, there is no right or wrong way to do it, as long as  it works for you.

replies.author: @UnmitigatedMind
replies.text: I agree and thank you for your input!

author: @brandonlaird6876
text:
I saw a flowchart for an IT tech. It went something like:
1) Look for a button that sounds like what you're trying to do -> 2) Click it -> 3) Did it work? (No) -> 4) Go back to step 1
When people say I'm good with computers, I always think of this and chuckle.

replies.author: @hotsnakes88
replies.text: This is my procedure for most things in life.
